attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-week history of increased anxiety, tremulousness, insomnia, palpitations, and increased bowel frequency. Six weeks ago, she was admitted to mental health after a failed suicide attempt by hanging herself with a noose. She was found by family prior to cardiac arrest and promptly brought into the hospital at that time. She promptly received a CT angiography of her head and neck which showed no damage to the vessels. She has a current medical history of anxiety, depression, PTSD and borderline personality disorder. She has never had any thyroid disease but her sister has Hashimoto's thyroiditis. She denies any flu-like symptoms, myalgia, or irritable/itchy/dry eyes. She has never been pregnant and has no previous cardiac history or history of lithium use. She does not take any cardiac medications.

On examination, she appears well with a pulse rate of 108/min, blood pressure 124/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. She is not diaphoretic but is warm to the touch, with no evidence of palmar erythema but with a fine tremor. There is no evidence of a goitre, no tenderness on palpation of the thyroid, no thyroid bruit identified, and no palpable nodule. There is no exophthalmos, proptosis, or lid lag.

Her current thyroid function tests are shown below. Prior to this, all her thyroid function tests have been normal.

  • TSH: < 0.01 (0.4-4 mU/L)

  • fT4: 27.5 (9-25 mU/L)

  • fT3: 10.1 (3.5-7.8 mU/L)

  • anti-TSH receptor antibody: negative

  • anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies: negative

  • anti-thyroglobulin antibody: negative

  • ESR: 10 mm/hr (0-20)

  • CRP: 3 mg/L (<5)

A nuclear medicine scan of the thyroid demonstrates uniformly reduced uptake.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Thyrotoxicosis refers to the clinical syndrome caused by an increased concentration of circulating free T3 and free T4 hormones. This patient is presenting with clear signs of thyrotoxicosis, indicated by her tremors, anxiety, palpitations, increased bowel frequency and warm peripheries. This is supported by her thyroid function tests which show a suppressed TSH and elevated free T3 and T4. There are many causes for thyrotoxicosis, the most common of which are causes of hyperthyroidism such as Graves disease, toxic multinodular goitre and toxic adenoma. Additional causes include those causing destruction of the thyroid gland such as subacute thyroiditis, drug-induced thyroiditis, and contrast-induced thyroiditis.

Graves disease is an autoimmune condition in which thyrotropin receptor autoantibodies stimulate the TSH receptor leading to an increase in the production and synthesis of thyroid hormone, causing hyperthyroidism and resulting in thyrotoxicosis. It is diagnosed by a suppressed TSH and increased free T4 and/or free T3 along with increased thyrotropin autoantibodies (TRAB). Although the patient has presented with symptoms of thyrotoxicosis and laboratory features of overt primary hyperthyroidism, the TRAB levels were in the normal range, making Graves disease less likely. However, antibody-negative Graves disease can occur. However, a nuclear medicine scan would show high uptake of the IV radiotracer in a diffuse pattern in Grave's disease.

Iodine contrast-induced thyrotoxicosis typically occurs in individuals with pre-existing thyroid conditions, most commonly nodular goitre (often due to residing in an iodine-deficient region), latent Graves disease, or toxic multinodular goitre. This phenomenon, known as the Jod Basedow effect, involves the development of a hyperthyroid state after receiving iodine contrast. This occurs in individuals with autonomous thyroid gland regions, and the administration of iodine activates these areas, leading to increased utilisation of iodine and subsequent thyroid hormone synthesis. It is worth noting that this response is pathological, and individuals with normal thyroid function should experience the opposite effect, resulting in a hypothyroid state known as the Wolff-Chaikoff effect.

Iodine contrast-induced thyrotoxicosis usually occurs 3-10 weeks after exposure to iodine and the severity is quite variable. The most likely cause of this patient’s presentation is iodine contrast-induced thyrotoxicosis as all the other main causes have been ruled out and there is a history of iodine contrast media (ICM) exposure along with symptoms and laboratory findings of hyperthyroidism. Most cases of iodine contrast-induced thyrotoxicosis resolve spontaneously after a few weeks. To differentiate between this and antibody-negative Graves disease, a thyroid scintigraphy can be done at least eight weeks after iodine contrast administration as the large amount of iodine within contrast media can prevent uptake of thyroid-specific radioisotopes into the thyroid for a while. In iodine-induced thyrotoxicosis, we would expect to see uniformly reduced uptake, whereas in Graves' disease or a toxic nodule, increased uptake would be observed.

Subacute thyroiditis occurs due to transient patchy inflammation of the thyroid gland and can be categorized as either De Quervain/granulomatous thyroiditis or subacute lymphocytic thyroiditis/postpartum thyroiditis. Subacute granulomatous thyroiditis commonly arises following a viral upper respiratory tract infection, while subacute lymphocytic thyroiditis can occur during the postnatal period or due to the use of medications like lithium, amiodarone, or alpha interferon. Both conditions progress through three phases, starting with hyperthyroidism, transitioning to hypothyroidism, and eventually returning to a euthyroid state. The thyrotoxic phase is characterised by fever, fatigue, myalgias, arthralgias, and a goitre (painful in granulomatous thyroiditis and painless in lymphocytic thyroiditis). The duration of the disease can range from a week to several months. Diagnosis is confirmed by evaluating the clinical history alongside altered thyroid function tests that correspond to the specific phase of subacute thyroiditis. Additionally, elevated ESR/CRP levels and decreased radioiodine uptake on thyroid scintigraphy are observed. However, this patient does not exhibit any history of flu-like symptoms, has no fever upon examination, and lacks evidence of a painful goitre or recent pregnancy. The laboratory tests also indicate a normal ESR. Hence, it is highly unlikely that this patient is suffering from subacute thyroiditis.

Tertiary hyperthyroidism is not a recognised medical condition.

Hashimoto's disease is the most prevalent form of autoimmune thyroiditis and the leading cause of hypothyroidism. The clinical presentation includes symptoms indicative of hypothyroidism, such as intolerance to cold temperatures, constipation, lethargy, weight gain, and the presence of a goitre. Laboratory investigations typically reveal an elevated level of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and decreased levels of free T4 and free T3, along with the presence of autoantibodies against thyroid peroxidase and thyroglobulin. In this particular case, the patient is highly unlikely to have Hashimoto's thyroiditis as she does not exhibit any symptoms consistent with hypothyroidism or show characteristic laboratory findings.

In conclusion, while iodine contrast-induced thyrotoxicosis is the most likely diagnosis based on the patient's history and presentation, a nuclear medicine scan is essential to definitively differentiate between this condition, a toxic nodule, and antibody-negative Graves' disease.
learningPoints: Iodine radiocontrast-induced thyrotoxicosis should be suspected in a thyrotoxic patient, particularly those with nodular goitre or thyroid nodule as these suggest autonomy, characteristic laboratory findings (overt or subclinical hyperthyroidism) and a history of iodine exposure.
A nuclear medicine scan is essential to differentiate between iodine-induced thyrotoxicosis, toxic nodule, and antibody-negative Graves' disease.
references: Ross DS, Burch HB, Cooper DS, Greenlee MC, Laurberg P, Maia AL, et al. 2016 American Thyroid Association Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis. Thyroid. 2016 Oct;26(10):1343–421.

‌Thyroid disease Using diagnostic tools effectively [Internet]. Australian Journal of General Practice. 2023 [cited 2023 Apr 20]. Available from: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2021/january-february/thyroid-disease-using-diagnostic-tools-effectively

Tomasz Bednarczuk, Thomas Heiberg Brix, Schima W, Georg Zettinig, Kahaly GJ. 2021 European Thyroid Association Guidelines for the Management of Iodine-Based Contrast Media-Induced Thyroid Dysfunction. 2021 Jul 1 [cited 2023 May 17];10(4):269–84. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8314764/
RANZCR Guidelines for Iodinated Contrast Administration. March 2018, RANZCR Website.
